summary: Spolsky critiques Munich's decision to replace Windows with Linux on 14,000 desktop computers, arguing that while it may work for basic tasks, it will fail for the many employees who depend on Windows-only software. He points out that Munich uses 175 custom Windows applications that would all need porting, plus commercial software with no Linux equivalent. He predicts that employees will quietly buy Windows at full retail price to get their jobs done, ultimately costing Munich more than a negotiated Microsoft deal would have.
key_points: Munich's 175 custom Windows applications represent a massive hidden migration cost that undermines the economic case for switching to Linux
Desktop OS migrations fail when they ignore the long tail of specialized software that individual workers depend on
Politically motivated technology decisions often result in paying twice: once for the mandated solution and once for the workaround that actually gets the job done
Bureaucratic organizations are particularly bad at surfacing migration failures because employees absorb dysfunction silently rather than reporting it
money_quotes: For a secretary who has to do email and type simple documents, that will probably be fine.
What about all the people who need to run software that doesn't run on Linux?
I guarantee you that somewhere out of those 14,000 desktops there's someone using Quark to publish an important report.

Every mid-level bureaucrat will realize they can't do their job because some application they need just doesn't run on Linux, and they'll buy Windows XP at full retail price, burying the costs in expense reports or petty cash or somewhere else.
Munich will start to realize that they're paying twice for software: once for the politically correct shelfware and once for the software they need to get their jobs done.
Either that or they'll simply be unable to do their jobs, and, like bureaucrats everywhere, they won't tell anyone, while the municipality quietly falls apart.
key_insight: Large-scale OS migrations driven by political rather than practical considerations fail because they ignore the long tail of specialized software dependencies, ultimately costing more than the system they replaced.
